title: These birds suck—literally
html_body:
Today in Science and science: A new treatment for neuropathic pain, cultivating lost crops, and more
Brought to you by the Gairdner Foundation
View this email in your browser
31 March 2026
Today’s Protostar is Natalie Mueller, one of the two inaugural winners of the Chrysalis Prize. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including birds that suck without cheeks and how discriminatory beliefs persist despite evidence of police misconduct.
pain  |  science translational medicine
Dulling the pain
Millions of Americans suffer from tingling, pain, and weakness in their hands and feet, a condition called peripheral neuropathy. The symptoms happen when the nerves that send signals between the central nervous system and the brain either send improper signals or fail to send the right signals. Current medications often bring limited pain relief, or confer serious side effects.

Researchers turned to the gene SCN9A, which codes for a specific sodium channel that controls pain signaling in pain-sensing neurons. They then engineered a protein that selectively shut down the gene, before packaging the protein into a virus that could deliver it when invading a subject’s cells. When tested on neurons derived from stem cells, the targeted gene was 70% less active. The treatment also provided pain relief to mice.

Finally, the researchers tested their therapy on crab-eating macaques for six months, finding that the gene was 50% less active in their nerve tissue. Promisingly, no brain or heart toxicity occurred; the researchers didn’t assess the monkeys’ pain relief due to ethical constraints.

The treatment provides an advantage over other methods that can accidentally target the wrong kinds of sodium channels in addition to the pain-sensing one. “Considering the lack of long-lasting and safe treatments for chronic neuropathic pain … genomic medicines may offer an attractive alternative approach for neuropathic pain,” explained the authors.
Read the Paper
Animals  |  news from Science
These birds suck—literally
A malachite sunbird (Nectarinia famosa) feeds on an aloe flower.  Nick garbutt/npl/minden
Butterflies suck—nectar. Walruses suck—clams. And most fishes suck everything they eat. But birds? Scientists had never seen suction—until now. In Current Biology, researchers report that sunbirds use suction to sip nectar. The find is also the first example of any vertebrate drinking through suction generated by the tongue alone, rather than by changing the shape of its mouth. “ That’s really pretty incredible ,” said evolutionary morphologist Kurt Schwenk, who was not involved in the study.

To learn how sunbirds feed, biologist David Cuban caught seven species of the animals in South Africa and Indonesia, filming their nectar feeding with a high-speed camera. He 3D printed artificial flowers and kept the base of the flower behind a screen so he could film the motion of the tongue without disturbing the birds. And because sunbird tongues are partially transparent, he could watch the nectar moving in the tongue.

After the sunbirds inserted their tongue into the flower, Cuban saw it moving up and down inside the beak. This motion creates suction, says Cuban, who led the new research as a Ph.D. student. The animals have a V-shaped trough that runs along the top side of their tongue. When they press the tongue against the roof of their beak, it flattens and the trough compresses. Pulling down on the tongue creates space between the upper bill and the trough, creating suction that draws in the nectar. The team ruled out other possibilities, such as capillary action pulling in the nectar, through computer modeling of the fluid dynamics.

Tongues are a powerful force in the history of life. Knowing the mechanism by which sunbirds use theirs to feed, Cuban and colleagues say, could help clarify how they coevolved with flowers and why they have a different feeding mechanism from hummingbirds.
Read the Full Story
political science  |  science advances
Minds mostly made up
Cases of alleged police misconduct have become headline news over the last several years, from the methods of the officers that detained and killed George Floyd to the tactics and use of force by U.S. ICE agents. Public reactions to such incidents are highly polarized—in no small part because they are not shaped by evidence alone: People’s prior beliefs can continue to color how they interpret events, even after clear findings emerge , a new study has found.

Researchers surveyed almost 16,000 people in Germany using an experimental scenario designed to mimic how real-world news unfolds. Participants first read an initial report of a violent incident involving police and an individual whose name was either "Mohamed Ahmed" or “Thomas Schneider.” They were later given additional information, including confirmation from an official investigation that police misconduct had occurred.

From the get-go, respondents were less likely to view the incident as police misconduct when the person involved had an immigrant-sounding name. This name-based disparity persisted, even when stronger evidence of misconduct was introduced. This pattern was driven by participants with moderate or extreme right-wing views, the team explained; they remained less likely to classify the incident as police misconduct when the victim was “Ahmed” despite being told that authorities had confirmed officers’ wrongdoing. The researchers describe the process as motivated reasoning, in which information is evaluated based on its consistency with existing values rather than its accuracy.

The authors conclude that among right-wing respondents, bias against immigrant victims of police misconduct proved “highly information resistant.” The findings highlight a broader challenge: Even clear evidence may not resolve public disagreement, helping explain why debates over policing can remain deeply polarized long after the facts are established.
Read the paper

Meet the 2026 laureates recognized for groundbreaking discoveries advancing human health, from fundamental biology to global health research, with advances that are transforming our understanding of disease and shaping the future of medicine.
Read Now
Protostar
courtesy of natalie mueller
Natalie Mueller
Assistant Professor of Anthropology, Washington University in St. Louis
Natalie Mueller is trying to rescue crops that civilization forgot. As an assistant professor of anthropology at Washington University in St. Louis, Mueller is one of the world’s leading experts on the “lost crops” of eastern North America—a suite of plants domesticated by Indigenous peoples thousands of years ago that today survive only as roadside weeds. Known in the archaeological record from storage pits, cave deposits, and ancient human feces, these plants were once staples of a sophisticated agricultural system stretching from the Appalachian Mountains to the Ozarks. Mueller wants to bring them back. This spring, the Cromwell Harbor Foundation named her one of two inaugural recipients of its Chrysalis Prize, awarding her $250,000 in unrestricted support for early-career scholars doing unusually imaginative, cross-disciplinary work.

Mueller’s work blends paleoethnobotany, morphometric analysis, experimental agriculture, and close collaboration with Indigenous communities. She maintains a seed bank of five lost crop species collected from across their original range, sharing them with researchers, educators, and Indigenous farmers who are beginning to experiment with growing them again. The questions animating her lab are deceptively simple: What can we learn from crops that people cultivated for millennia before losing them? And what might we recover?

ScienceAdviser spoke with Mueller about her work. Below is that conversation, edited for brevity.

How did you end up in this field?

I got into archaeology through something completely different. At first, I was really interested in what is true from ancient stories, anything from the Old Testament to Greek mythology. What could we check the accuracy of? How could we verify things that have been passed down across cultures? That inspired me to start taking archaeology classes as an undergrad, and then I just really loved it. In one of the introductory classes, I read a paper by my mentor, Gayle Fritz, about the lost crops. I thought it was the coolest thing ever.

No one had really ever published anything trying to cultivate them. So, a lot of my research has just been taking these plants from the wild and trying to cultivate them in garden settings, replicating some of the things I think ancient people might have been doing. And if you could go back in time and interview ancient farmers in eastern North America, they probably would think it was kind of silly how much we focus on just these five plants. I think the diversity of things they were cultivating and taking care of and eating was really enormous. They’d be kind of entertained by how obsessed we’ve become with just their annual seed crops.

What exactly are the lost crops?

They’re a suite of crops that were cultivated in eastern North America by Indigenous people in the ancient past. Some of the crops that were cultivated by people in this region are still grown; sunflowers and squashes were domesticated here. But there are a bunch of others that really only exist today in the form of weeds on the landscape. Those are the lost crops. We know about them only from the archaeological record, since they don’t really appear much even in early written records or oral histories.

The smoking gun that convinced everyone that people were eating them was the paleofeces. I read about this as an undergrad and became just fascinated. These plants had this really long history with people, were so useful, and were just kind of hiding in plain sight on the landscape, waiting for us to notice them again.

Has working with Indigenous communities changed how you think about the plants themselves?

Absolutely. Something I really didn’t have a firm grasp on before I spent a lot of time talking with my Indigenous collaborators is that they literally see seeds—particularly the maize they steward, but seeds in general—as kin. So, it’s really important to them that the seeds are treated respectfully and as living creatures, rather than as objects or artifacts, which is the way archaeologists would often treat them. That goes for the modern seeds in my seed bank, but also for the archaeological seeds in museums.

It took me a long time to wrap my head around, but now that I finally get it, it really affects not only the way I approach research and which questions are appropriate, but also the way I think about domestication. Because if people saw it that way in the past too—which they probably did, since they were the ancestors of these same people—then they may have been interacting with plants in a really different way than we do today. And that actually resonates with observations I was already making: These plants are incredibly plastic. If you change something about their environment, they change in response in really obvious ways. I think that was rightly interpreted by people in the past as a marker of their agency.

Do you grow any of them yourself?

I love gardening, and if I could spend all day gardening, I would. If I ever get fired from my academic job, that’s where you’ll find me. I do kind of grow the lost crops at my house, but it’s by accident. They’re following me. I’m serious, it’s weird. I probably accidentally introduced them to my house garden in the city, so they grow there every year— and I don’t weed them out, obviously. And I have a little place outside St. Louis in the country, woods mostly, and a couple of them occur there too. I don’t think I introduced those, I think they just happen to be there. They’re native plants, so they pop up all over the place, but it’s still always neat to see them growing so close to home.

What will you do with the prize money?

One thing I’m looking into is how I can invest part of it to create basically yearly dividends that I can spend on research or use to support my graduate students, as opposed to spending it all right now on one project. Especially right now, we have a ton of uncertainty about where we’re going to get funding moving forward. So much has been cut from federal funding, and in particular our graduate students have lost access to NSF dissertation improvement grants. That’s how most archaeology graduate students funded their dissertations. We don’t get big enough grants in archaeology to really support graduate students the way a lot of natural scientists do. I just want to make sure I don’t squander this and that I can use it to generate the ability to do research moving forward.

This means war
An expansive new law promises to broaden China’s “war” on light pollution and other environmental problems, but critics worry the measure will also curtail the public’s ability to challenge harmful government projects.
Read more at ScienceInsider
In the dark
Cuban scientists and doctors are struggling to cope with power blackouts caused by a U.S. blockade of oil deliveries.
Read more at ScienceInsider
The cost of e-cigarettes
A sweeping review of research published between 2017 and 2025 provides the strongest evidence yet that vaping is likely to cause lung and oral cancer.
Carcinogenesis Paper  |  Read more at The Guardian
Sometimes the most fulfilling academic life is not the one we first imagined, but the one we build through both intentional choices and unexpected experiences.
WORKING LIFE  |  26 March 2026  |  Ravirajsinh Jadeja
Last but not least
I struggle to make sense of quantum mechanics even at the best of times, so I don’t envy the physicists attempting to determine how the quantum world works in an expanding universe. Luckily, these scientists are getting some help from black holes.
Phie Jacobs, General Assignment Reporter, Science

With contributions from Hannah Richter, Erik Stokstad, and Ana Georgescu

items.title: Considering the lack of long-lasting and safe treatments for chronic neuropathic pain … genomic medicines may offer an attractive alternative approach for neuropathic pain
sunbirds use suction to sip nectar
People’s prior beliefs can continue to color how they interpret events, even after clear findings emerge
these scientists are getting some help from black holes
